speaker: interviewer
text: When your children come back, and your grandchildren, are they going to come in a back way, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably, they usually do. Well, it's only one will come. The big boy-- Rochelle can't come back, because the little one coming from school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you talking about the logistics of having somebody meet them there?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I just wondered because I heard--

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of noise?

speaker: interviewer
text: One of your grandchildren saying 4:30, so I just wanted to--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on. Hold on. This is reel number four with Judith Rubinstein. You were starting to tell me about the composition of the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was almost like a little United Nations, with people from all over-- different tongues, different nationalities, and different badges. The Jews wore the yellow stars, the yellow triangle. And there were the murderers from wherever, with the red triangle, the homosexuals, the Seventh Day Adventists. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] From different tongues. And we were into the same problem, but still, the-- the Jews got the brunt, because we were the biggest part. And there-- we were the-- the biggest-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, the largest group. So they really didn't need it, as-- all that workers. They didn't know what to do with us. They shipped, from time to time, a-- a group out to Germany to work in factories. But by that time, the trains didn't travel-- go so well. The war was very close. So they try to eliminate as much-- as many as it can. I had a good friend-- she was a social democrat from Germany, a Catholic woman. Her own brother sent her there because he was a Nazi. And he was embarrassed by her being different. So she suffered. It was many--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you become--how did you become friends with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Working side by side, in that old building. And then I had a friend from Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name? That woman who had been sent by her brother who was a Nazi?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember names. I'm sorry. 50 years is a long time. Just, I remember her face, I still see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. In Auschwitz, well, they-- were just picked to another kommando. We never see the same person again. They sent us from one block to the other one. And that was it-- like a different world all over again. New people, new faces.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a difference in your treatment as a Jew from treatment of other prisoners who were not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. Well, and the Polish women and the Gentiles, they felt superior. They felt-- they got a little bit of a-- first of all, they were strong, healthy. And only the stronger, the fittest survived. And they had some-- some had jobs over us. They were supervisors. And everybody was-- who had a little bit better than the other-- just-- just took advantage of it. For a slice of bread, or for anything. I never-- I came out from a very modest background. I never heard of-- I mean heard, but I never encountered a homosexual, a-- a lesbian. And there, it was rampant, by the Nazis, especially. The SS women, they were just impossible. And hitting, and killing, and shooting. This was the first 1,000 prostitutes they picked up in Berlin on the street. They became the SS-- over-- the one-- the supervisors. So they dress them up in a uniform, and they gave them a-- a gun. So they were trigger happy. They became somebodies. And they were really vicious. Bad. I never met a good one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- were you victimized by them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Luckily, I was just one of the thousands. I was lucky, I guess. I was lucky. I never-- I once, in Germany, I was beaten up. But, of course, no fault of my own. And I had surgery a few years ago, because there was a twisted bone there. But otherwise--

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was when? Was that when you were at Ravensbruck?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was Malchow, while we were in the working camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so let's just go back to your work at Auschwitz. You had finished with the slave labor.

speaker: interviewee
text: The most painful job came after that, when we finished up that old building. I-- I was very unhappy. I didn't want to stay in the camp. So when they came, this old Wehrmacht soldier, he was an elderly man, looking for workers to-- made a group of 20 women. And I, of course, I wanted to go. So he picked me, and took us out to clean the watchtowers around the crematorium. And the four corners, four-- five girls to a corner-- to a tower. That was four corners. We were supposed to wash the floor and wash the windows, and clean up. It wasn't much to do. Now he, as we find out later on, he made this kommando for his own interest. He was getting out the hard liquor to the crematorium, to the people, to the sonderkommando who was working there. And in return for the favor, because these guys couldn't work-- I mean, it was just unbelievable, painful, to see. Sometimes they find their own relatives among the dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the work of the sonderkommando.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were the one who had to separate the bodies, and put them into the oven and burn them. And the ashes, whatever-- I don't know. If I would know exactly, I wouldn't be able to tell you, because nobody came out from there. I can just tell you what I saw over the fence from the watchtower. We saw what went on in the yard, in the front of the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this guy, this Wehrmacht soldier, he was-- he was not bad. He didn't hurt us, but he had his own interest, why he-- he did what he did. He carried out the liquor-- nobody checked him over. And he sold it for this sonderkommando, this man who worked inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for the favor, for-- he got all the jewelries and gold from the teeth, what they pulled out from the dead bodies, and whatever they find in the clothes. Everybody brought something with them. And they used to take us back in the evening to the camp with a bulging attache case, full with valuables.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he made a good business out of  it. He occasionally-- he asked that-- what's the matter? Why are you here in that horrible place? So, well, we tell-- we told him that we are-- because we are Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, don't tell me that. I can't believe it. It's just too horrible. You must have done something terrible that gets such a punishment. He said, don't look there. Don't look on the bodies. Pray for Hitler's life, then you will survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this man SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was a Wehrmacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how was it that Wehrmacht was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Wehrmacht was the regular army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So how--

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was old, already. He was over 60. So he couldn't go to fight in the front. So they used him of these kind of a duties, the guard-- guard duties.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this at the time at Auschwitz when there was a reduction in the younger SS, and there was an increase in older Wehrmacht, and home guard, and Hitlerjunge, and things like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, because the young ones were out fighting in the Russian front. So they replaced them. And they were-- some were vicious, but they were a little bit more mellow. Age-- they were not as young anymore. And we had no big problem with him, as long as he did his job. But he got into-- us into trouble. I don't know if I have time to tell you the story. While we were cleaning these watchtowers, it wasn't much to-- to-- really, to work there. And what we saw, it was very painful, as the people were walking toward the gate. And we knew that where are they going. And from these windows, we saw what's going on in the yard in the front of the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: From what windows?

speaker: interviewee
text: The watch-- watchtower, the what we were cleaning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would climb up into the watchtower?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- it was, what, one floor above. And there was windows, all four sides, all windows. So we could, from wherever we looked, we saw what's going on-- from the train, as they walked in, until the gate, and into the crematorium we had. And it was very painful to see. And the chimney was going, day and night. And then, when they couldn't eliminate all these bodies so fast, then we saw the piles on-- on the yard, waiting to be burned. And many of them had to be carried out. It was a forest right next to the crematorium-- they called it the forbidden forest-- where they had fires. They were burning the bodies. And so one day, it was-- that was in the middle of September, it was-- the weather was still nice. And the blackberries started to grow in-- on the bushes. And we knew that, if we can smuggle in some of this fruit, we can sell it for bread, what was-- was the-- really the-- one of the most precious commodity. And so, as we cleaned up the tower fast. And then the five of us started to pick the berries. And as we walked into the forest, the berries were bigger and bigger. All of the sudden, it was full, the dish, what we carried with us. And that was our only possession we had at around wall, that we had to carry under our arm. And we came out from the forest, and the head of the crematorium-- the SS-- noticed us, and called us over. And through the fence, we had to give in our fruit. And before we turned around, an SS soldier was behind us, and took us in, back to the working camp, where we-- the sleeping quarters. And there was a big platform-- they called the platz, where it was all the activities went on. We're standing there for a while. And from the number, they find out who I am. And within a half an hour came the-- the sentence that the five of us, we wanted to run away-- and for that, the punishment is hanging by the neck. The following morning, 10 o'clock, it's going to be a public hanging in that place. And they dismissed us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was numb. I had no one who should cry for me. But my friends-- one had two sisters, the other one had one sister-- they went hysterical, and they started to run around. What can you do? Nobody had an answer. So it was in a Friday, and it was just before the Shabbat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our block-- block leader, she was a Slovak, from Slovakia, girl. Her name was Alice. She said, go over to the other block. There is a woman who is here for a long time. Maybe-- maybe she has some answer for you. So we smuggled ourselves-- the gate was still open. Because between one block and the other one, was a big gate, and they closed it up for the evening.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was still open, and we went-- walked over, and knocked on her door. She had a little cubicle to herself, because she was the block leader, from Slovakia, a very fine woman. She was about 35. In our eyes, she looked old. And she said, yes. I heard all about it. You don't have to tell me. I know what happened. Sit down, let's pray first. And she just lit the Shabbat candle. It was unbelievable, that in Auschwitz, someone should dare to do that. And that was for certain death if  they would catch her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we said the prayers. And then she said, unfortunately, not me, and nobody else, can do a thing for you. This is something is in the Nazis' hand. And they decided that you wanted to run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody-- it was no trial. They never ask witnesses. That's how it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So go with peace. Let me bless you, and go with peace. And you will meet your loved one in the other side. So it wasn't very comforting. And it was just numb, like-- it's just, we didn't know what's going on. We just walked back to the sleeping quarters. And that night, certainly was no sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: Early in the morning came a young woman. They were called the [NON-ENGLISH], they were the liaison between anything-- was a-- was a messenger, a messengers. They were the one who were sent from one camp to other one. Came one with an order, from our leader, that for five gold watches, he will try to save our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we were positive that this woman-- this-- her name was Ethel-- she had some doing, to do with it. As we figured out later, that our leader, the-- the Wehrmacht soldier, probably was embarrassed that they might ask him that where was he, that he let us walk around in the forbidden territory? So he was embarrassed, and he wanted to eliminate the whole problem. So the five gold watches we find within a few minutes. There was no problem. And it was given to whom, I don't know, just we were told by our block leader that we should never go in that side of the forest again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, certainly we didn't. About two weeks later, it was the uprising in the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you get to that, I'd like you to just give me some more details of what you witnessed when you would work in the watchtowers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we saw as the people walked in on the gate, and they were sitting on the grass, in the front of the building. There were a couple of young boys who were tending-- there were chickens and rabbits in cages. It was beautifully landscaped-- the front, flowers, that people shouldn't get panicky. And the children were playing, with-- were watching the animals until their turn came. So they didn't hear anything. They didn't see anything. Because once transport was taken in, that was it. They didn't know what happened to them. They're just waiting for their turn. And we saw them, while walking in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever witness any resistance or any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. How could you? They're just innocent people, without arm-- old and young, very young, babies, and old sick people. And they were getting the gun. So it's-- wouldn't do any good. It's not just that, if somebody felt offensive, but everybody else was punished. For one who did something wrong, then everybody else was punished for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I escaped death at that time. But I witnessed a public hanging before. I just want to spend just a couple minutes more talking about what you witnessed when you worked near the sonderkommando. And then what would happen? That group of people would be congregated outside the doors waiting, and then what would--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- and then they disappeared. And then the following day, or a few hours later, we saw that the the-- clothes was taken out in the bundles, and they were sending it to [NON-ENGLISH], where they were sorting it out. And make parcels, and send it away. Or give it to the inmates-- because we were 30,000 women, you know, needed clothes all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever witness the SS putting the gas into the outlets on top of the gas chambers, or anything like that? Did you ever see any of that taking place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was indoors. If we would go, then I would never make it. I just remember there was a staircase going down. And that's why I have this aversion of the stairs. I-- I-- whenever I see a staircase going down, with a-- with a railing in the side, I just can't-- I can't step on it. Because it brings back memories. But the rest, we didn't see. Just we saw their bodies-- their naked bodies the following few hours later, or the following day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to have any contact with any of the men in the sonderkommando?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Matter of fact, one man-- his name was Press, Efraim Press. He went to school with me. I didn't talk to him, but I knew he was there. But there was another man, his was Leo Horowitz, and he was newly married. And we were sleeping in the same room in the ghetto, and we got on the same train, and we arrived the same time. His wife, her name was Ellie, Elli Spindler. She was working with us on our old building crew. And when I was taken to clean the watchtowers, one day, I noticed, I-- I noticed-- the husband, Leo, working in the crematorium. And we started to talk-- you know, between the Nazis disappear, hear a snatch of conversation and there. And, surely, he was working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I ask him, tell me, is it true what they say that's happening in those? He said, unfortunately, it is true. But if I think about my parents-- had this horrible fate, I can't accept it. But, unfortunately, people are killed here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he used to send messages with us to his wife and little packages.

speaker: interviewer
text: When he was working in the sonderkommando?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to have contact with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was the watchtower was so close-- like the window from here. And it was just-- just we-- from the watchtower, we talked.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there were guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or we went down-- we went down the main floor. And there was a fence between us. But in an odd moments, when there was not Nazis around, we could talk. Just here and there, a snatch of conversation. And he sent us with a little stone, wrapped in a stone to throw over a little message, what we smuggled into his wife. And one day we took the wife out. Somebody from us, and stayed home. And she came out, and they saw each other for a few minutes. And then came the uprising. Unfortunately, Leo was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one more thing before you get to that. From that vantage point, when you would talk to him through the fence, were you able to see into the crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- no, it was over a big iron gate. And we were on the angle. Here was the wall, it was the door, and we were-- we were-- in this corner, it was the tower. So we just thought, what is going on in the yard as they were marching by the fence, inside to the yard?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were about to tell me about a uprising.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- after that, I never went-- I mean, if I could help it. I was cleaning the other side of-- another corner. And then one day, that was exactly the 7th-- I checked it out, and I studied books-- 7th of October was an uprising in the crematorium. It was started quietly in the morning. We washed the windows. And we sat down, and we were talking, praying. I was very religious, and I was never so religious in my life. I was just full with hope that we will make it. Unbelievable, but that's how it was. And while we were sitting on the floor, we didn't want to see what's going on, because the wood was about this high, and from there with the windows. And we-- we didn't wanted to be seen. And all of the sudden, we hear a big noise, and peeked out in the window. We saw that, by the fence, these guys, the sonderkommando with axe and with shovel, they're banging on the fence. And the fence is falling down. And we just-- we were just stunned. We looked at each other, and a minute, later there was a big flame came out from the crematorium. We fell on our stomach. I was shaking like a leaf, and didn't dare to look. And by that time, the Nazis were shooting and yelling. I started to run around, and men were running. And they who were working, side-- it was a field, they were working there. And when they saw something going on, they started to run. I understand one person made it, then ran away. But the rest were shot, unfortunately. And then where we were lying there, shaking, all of the sudden an SS came up, and was surprised to see what are you doing. And we were trying to explain that we are cleaning the towers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took us in right away, made the 20 girls in a kommando, and marched to the-- and we had to go through the big gate where was their office building. And our leader had to give the-- the-- the order that 20 girls in-- and what they were working. And he's taking them back to their-- to their sleeping quarters.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we heard as the head of the SS-- SS said, [GERMAN]. They saw too much, they have to go. So we-- we were sure that this is the end of us. But it didn't work out. About a day or two, we stayed in the camp. I didn't know much what happened. We-- others knew as much-- we find out that it was a group of women who were worked in they called a union-- in the leader union. In Auschwitz proper was a munition factory, and there were girls, Jewish girls who were working there, went out every day was-- and Auschwitz was 3 kilometers from Birkenau. And little by little, these girls smuggled in the gunpowder-- tiny bits, bits by bits-- until they find the place, they find the route into the crematorium. And I don't know that, because I heard from people that it's true. The others, they don't know-- because so few survivors, that while the planning went on, as the partisans were in the forest, Polish and Jewish partisans-- and they were supposed to come out when the crematorium is blown up, come out and liberate Auschwitz. Unfortunately, something went wrong. SS find out too early, and they were just waiting. They watched what's happening. And when the crematorium blow up-- was blown up, it was in big flame-- they forced these sonderkommando, these people, to go into the burning building. Of course, they refused. So they were shot. Nobody-- nobody survived from them-- as far as I know. And then a few days later, we were just sent to the bathhouse, and was disinfected again-- gave us fresh clothes, and took us out to the railway station with a loaf of bread, and closed the door, and we were on-- we're on our way. We didn't know that-- where to. We never hoped that we will get out from there. And they took us. For about two days we traveled. And in Berlin, the train stopped for a minute or so. And they took us in the siding. And they following day we arrived to a camp-- Ravensbruck. Well, it was a great welcoming committee, didn't wait for us. And the first two days we were spending in a tent, with no food. And it was raining, and this was-- I still have a rheumatism, I feel in my bones. We were sleeping on the frozen ground, without any cover. And we got a coat in Ravensbruck-- a coat. And after three weeks being under observation, there I always had a-- a mischief. There were a lot of lice there. And we were lying-- by that time it was the end of November, and it was cold, and was-- we went-- just went out to the roll call in the evening, morning and the evening, And nothing to do during the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're lying in our bed, and this started the lice epidemic. And I scratched my stomach. And this was itchy. And I started to scratch more. And I had a red spot there. So then one day, they-- we were gathered for an inspection, took us into the hospital. And they were recruiting a group of women-- 1,000 women-- to send to-- I didn't know at that time where-- to go to work in a factory. So when the doctor came to me, he saw the red spots, put me on the side. That's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends knew that, if I will be left in Ravensbruck alone, I will never make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they get a contact with a Hungarian woman was working there in the hospital, who's Mengele had made experiment on, and gave her male-- male hormones. And this woman grew a long, black beard. Her voice changed, and she was miserable. She didn't have anything to shave with. And she felt full with hatred and misery. So my-- one of my friends find out that she's working there, went to her, and said, listen, if we leave our friend here, she will never make it. Can you do something? She said, what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after got my name, she went and she was cleaning there. And when the Nazis weren't around, she just pulled out my file from one batch, and pulled out someone else's, and put mine back to the one who went in the transport. And she send a message to me that I should be out with the group at 6:00 in the morning, before they are starting to send the transport away. I had a good pair of shoes. I give it to her, and gave me a wooden clog instead. I took my-- I had a good sweater, and my friends, also, whatever they had, give it to her because we-- that was we could pay her kindness. And I will stay here, out in the morning, and the whole night I didn't sleep. And when they finally called my name, I was so relieved that now, maybe, I have a chance to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name? Do you know that woman's name? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just I know she was from Hungary. We-- we didn't know names, which was just faces, you know, just friends. So they took us on a train. We traveled the whole day, and full-- and then at nighttime, we arrived to a station, and it was about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just stop you for a moment? That time that were in Ravensbruck did you work in there at all? You were just waiting.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were under observation. No. Well, there was something that I remember very clearly. Before we--
